questions.baseline_answer:
The REALM (Retrieval-Augmented Language Model) retrieves from an external textual knowledge source, which is a corpus of text passages. This corpus serves as an explicit source of world knowledge that the model accesses during pre-training. Unlike models that rely solely on stored knowledge within their parameters, REALM uses a learned textual knowledge retriever to find relevant information from this external corpus. The retrieved knowledge is then incorporated into the language model pre-training process, thereby explicitly using the external corpus to improve the model's understanding and generation capabilities [1][2][3][4][5].

Citations:  
[1][2][3][4][5] data/2002.08909v1.pdf (chunks 84.0, 85.0, 96.0, 115.0, 120.0)
questions.orchestrator_answer: REALM retrieves information from an external textual knowledge source by using a learned textual knowledge retriever integrated into its language model pre-training process [E1][E2]. This approach explicitly exposes the role of world knowledge by requiring the model to query and retrieve relevant documents during training, rather than embedding all knowledge solely within its parameters [E3][E4]. The retrieved external knowledge is used by the model to assist its predictions, providing access to relevant text from an external corpus; however, the evidence does not explicitly detail how the retrieved knowledge enhances predictions or whether it is accessed directly at output generation [E1][E5].

questions.orchestrator_answer: The paper evaluates REALM by reporting both end-to-end results and the frequency at which the gold answer appears in the top-5 retrievals prior to any fine-tuning [E1][E4][E5]. This evaluation metric is designed specifically to isolate the contribution of improvements made to the retriever during pre-training, independently from the effects of end-to-end fine-tuning [E2]. The frequency of the gold answer in the top-5 retrievals serves as a key measure to assess retrieval performance before fine-tuning [E3]. This metric highlights the impact of the retriever component specifically, emphasizing the retriever’s improvement achieved during pre-training [E4][E5]. The supplied evidence does not include details about particular downstream tasks or named benchmarks used for evaluation beyond these retrieval-focused metrics.

questions.baseline_answer:
REALM, which stands for Retrieval-Augmented Language Model pre-training, is described as a novel framework that enhances language model pre-training algorithms by integrating a learned textual knowledge retriever. Unlike traditional models that embed knowledge solely within their parameters, REALM explicitly uses a retrieval mechanism to expose the role of world knowledge more directly to the model during training. This approach allows the model to access and leverage external textual data dynamically rather than relying only on internally stored knowledge in the model's parameters [1][2][3][4][5].

questions.orchestrator_answer: REALM, which stands for Retrieval-Augmented Language Model, is a language model pre-training method that enhances traditional language model training by integrating a learned textual knowledge retriever [E1]. This approach augments standard language model pre-training algorithms by explicitly involving a retriever that accesses relevant information from a text corpus during training [E2]. Unlike models that embed world knowledge solely within their parameters, REALM explicitly exposes the role of world knowledge by requiring the model to retrieve relevant information as part of its prediction process [E3]. Therefore, retrieval-augmented language model pre-training refers to the practice of combining language model training with an active, learned retrieval mechanism to access and utilize external textual knowledge, rather than relying only on knowledge stored implicitly in model parameters [E4][E5].

arXiv:2002.08909v1 [cs.CL] 10 Feb 2020
REALM: Retrieval-Augmented Language Model Pre-Training
Kelvin Guu * 1 Kenton Lee * 1 Zora T ung1 Panupong Pasupat 1 Ming-Wei Chang 1
Abstract
Language model pre-training has been shown to
capture a surprising amount of world knowledge,
crucial for NLP tasks such as question answer-
ing. However, this knowledge is stored implic-
itly in the parameters of a neural network, requir-
ing ever-larger networks to cover more facts. To
capture knowledge in a more modular and inter-
pretable way, we augment language model pre-
training with a latent knowledge retriever, which
allows the model to retrieve and attend over doc-
uments from a large corpus such as Wikipedia,
used during pre-training, ﬁne-tuning and infer-
ence. For the ﬁrst time, we show how to pre-
train such a knowledge retriever in an unsuper-
vised manner, using masked language model-
ing as the learning signal and backpropagating
through a retrieval step that considers millions
of documents. We demonstrate the effective-
ness of Retrieval-Augmented Language Model
pre-training (REALM) by ﬁne-tuning on the chal-
lenging task of Open-domain Question Answer-
ing (Open-QA). We compare against state-of-the-
art models for both explicit and implicit knowl-
edge storage on three popular Open-QA bench-
marks, and ﬁnd that we outperform all previous
methods by a signiﬁcant margin (4-16% absolute
accuracy), while also providing qualitative bene-
ﬁts such as interpretability and modularity.
1. Introduction
Recent advances in language model pre-training have
shown that models such as BERT (
Devlin et al., 2018),
RoBERTa ( Liu et al., 2019) and T5 ( Raffel et al., 2019)
store a surprising amount of world knowledge, ac-
quired from the massive text corpora they are trained
on (
Petroni et al., 2019). For example, BERT is able to

In these language models, the learned world knowledge is
stored implicitly in the parameters of the underlying neural

To capture knowledge in a more interpretable and modular

retain knowledge from millions of documents, since a ques-
tion could be about any of them.
We focus on Open-QA systems that utilize a textual knowl-
edge corpus Z as the knowledge source. Many of these
systems employ a retrieval-based approach: given a ques-
tion x, retrieve potentially relevant documents z from
the corpus Z, and then extract an answer y from the
documents (
Brill et al., 2002; Chen et al. , 2017; Lee et al. ,
2019). Our approach, REALM, is inspired by this
paradigm and extends it to language model pre-training.
Alternatively, some recent work has proposed generation-
based systems that apply a sequence-to-sequence model on
x to directly generate y token-by-token (Lewis et al., 2019;
Raffel et al., 2019). We will compare against state-of-the-
art systems from both paradigms in our experiments.
3. Approach
We start by formalizing REALM’s pre-training and ﬁne-
tuning tasks as a retrieve-then-predict generative process
in Section
3.1. Then in Section 3.2, we describe the model
architectures for each component of that process. In Sec-
tion
3.3, we show how to implement REALM pre-training
and ﬁne-tuning by maximizing the likelihood of REALM’s
generative process. En route, we address important compu-
tational challenges, explain why training works, and also
discuss strategies for injecting useful inductive biases. The
overall framework is illustrated in Figure
2.
3.1. REALM’s generative process
For both pre-training and ﬁne-tuning, REALM takes some
input x and learns a distribution p(y |x) over possible out-
puts y. For pre-training, the task is masked language mod-
eling: x is a sentence from a pre-training corpus X with
some tokens masked out, and the model must predict the
value of those missing tokens, y. For ﬁne-tuning, the task
is Open-QA: x is a question, and y is the answer.
REALM decomposes p(y |x) into two steps: retrieve, then
predict. Given an input x, we ﬁrst retrieve possibly helpful
documents z from a knowledge corpus Z. We model this as
a sample from the distribution p(z |x). Then, we condition
on both the retrieved z and the original input x to generate
the output y—modeled as p(y |z, x). To obtain the overall
likelihood of generating y, we treat z as a latent variable
and marginalize over all possible documents z, yielding
p(y |x) =

p(y |z, x) p(z |x). (1)
3.2. Model architecture
We now describe the two key components: the
neural knowledge retriever , which models p(z |x),
and the knowledge-augmented encoder , which models
p(y |z, x).
Knowledge Retriever The retriever is deﬁned using a
dense inner product model:
p(z |x) = exp f (x, z)

f (x, z) = Embedinput(x)⊤ Embeddoc(z),
where Embedinput and Embeddoc are embedding functions
that map x and z respectively to d-dimensional vectors.
The relevance score f (x, z) between x and z is deﬁned as
the inner product of the vector embeddings. The retrieval
distribution is the softmax over all relevance scores.
We implement the embedding functions using BERT-style
Transformers (

questions.baseline_answer:
REALM differs from a standard parametric language model such as BERT in that it does not solely store knowledge in its parameters. Instead, REALM augments language model pre-training algorithms with a learned textual knowledge retriever. This means that rather than embedding all world knowledge internally within model parameters, REALM explicitly accesses external knowledge sources through retrieval mechanisms during processing. This design explicitly exposes the role of world knowledge by allowing the model to query a knowledge base as part of its language modeling process [1][2][3][4][5].

Citations:  
[1][2][3][4][5] data/2002.08909v1.pdf
questions.orchestrator_answer: REALM differs from a standard parametric language model such as BERT by separating where and how knowledge is stored and accessed [E1][E2][E4][E5]. Standard models like BERT store world knowledge implicitly within their neural network parameters, embedding information directly into the model weights, which makes this knowledge difficult to explicitly access or interpret [E3]. This implicit storage imposes scalability limitations because capturing more facts requires increasingly larger networks, which also reduces interpretability [E3]. In contrast, REALM introduces a learned textual knowledge retriever that explicitly accesses external documents from a large corpus such as Wikipedia during pre-training, fine-tuning, and inference [E3]. This retrieval mechanism allows the model to obtain relevant textual information on demand rather than relying solely on internal parameter storage [E1][E2][E4][E5]. Consequently, knowledge is stored externally in a modular corpus, making the model’s knowledge acquisition more interpretable and scalable [E2][E3].

questions.question: How does REALM's retrieve-then-predict framework work, including the role of the latent document variable z?

[Page 3]
REALM: Retrieval-Augmented Language Model Pre-Training
els comprehend a single document, Open-QA models must
retain knowledge from millions of documents, since a ques-
tion could be about any of them.
We focus on Open-QA systems that utilize a textual knowl-
edge corpus Z as the knowledge source. Many of these
systems employ a retrieval-based approach: given a ques-
tion x, retrieve potentially relevant documents z from
the corpus Z, and then extract an answer y from the
documents (
Brill et al., 2002; Chen et al. , 2017; Lee et al. ,
2019). Our approach, REALM, is inspired by this
paradigm and extends it to language model pre-training.
Alternatively, some recent work has proposed generation-
based systems that apply a sequence-to-sequence model on
x to directly generate y token-by-token (Lewis et al., 2019;
Raffel et al., 2019). We will compare against state-of-the-
art systems from both paradigms in our experiments.
3. Approach
We start by formalizing REALM’s pre-training and ﬁne-
tuning tasks as a retrieve-then-predict generative process
in Section
3.1. Then in Section 3.2, we describe the model
architectures for each component of that process. In Sec-
tion
3.3, we show how to implement REALM pre-training
and ﬁne-tuning by maximizing the likelihood of REALM’s
generative process. En route, we address important compu-
tational challenges, explain why training works, and also
discuss strategies for injecting useful inductive biases. The
overall framework is illustrated in Figure
2.
3.1. REALM’s generative process
For both pre-training and ﬁne-tuning, REALM takes some
input x and learns a distribution p(y |x) over possible out-
puts y. For pre-training, the task is masked language mod-
eling: x is a sentence from a pre-training corpus X with
some tokens masked out, and the model must predict the
value of those missing tokens, y. For ﬁne-tuning, the task
is Open-QA: x is a question, and y is the answer.
REALM decomposes p(y |x) into two steps: retrieve, then
predict. Given an input x, we ﬁrst retrieve possibly helpful
documents z from a knowledge corpus Z. We model this as
a sample from the distribution p(z |x). Then, we condition
on both the retrieved z and the original input x to generate
the output y—modeled as p(y |z, x). To obtain the overall
likelihood of generating y, we treat z as a latent variable
and marginalize over all possible documents z, yielding
p(y |x) =
∑
z∈Z
p(y |z, x) p(z |x). (1)
3.2. Model architecture
We now describe the two key components: the
neural knowledge retriever , which models p(z |x),
and the knowledge-augmented encoder , which models
p(y |z, x).
Knowledge Retriever The retriever is deﬁned using a
dense inner product model:
p(z |x) = exp f (x, z)
∑
z′ exp f (x, z′) ,
f (x, z) = Embedinput(x)⊤ Embeddoc(z),
where Embedinput and Embeddoc are embedding functions
that map x and z respectively to d-dimensional vectors.
The relevance score f (x, z) between x and z is deﬁned as
the inner product of the vector embeddings. The retrieval
distribution is the softmax over all relevance scores.
We implement the embedding functions using BERT-style
Transformers (
Devlin et al., 2018). Following standard
practices, we join spans of text by applying wordpiece tok-
enization, separating them with [SEP] tokens, preﬁxing a
[CLS] token, and appending a ﬁnal [SEP] token.

As in
Devlin et al. (2018), we pass this into a Transformer,
which produces one vector for each token, including the
vector corresponding to [CLS] which is used as a “pooled”
representation of the sequence (denoted BERTCLS). Finally,
we perform a linear projection to reduce the dimensionality
of the vector, denoted as a projection matrix W:
Embedinput(x) = WinputBERTCLS(joinBERT(x))
Embeddoc(z) = WdocBERTCLS(joinBERT(ztitle, zbody))
where ztitle is the document’s title and zbody is its body.

While asynchronous refreshes can be used for both pre-
training and ﬁne-tuning, in our experiments we only use it
for pre-training. For ﬁne-tuning, we just build the MIPS in-

3 Note that we still ﬁne-tune Embedinput,
so the retrieval function is still updated from the query sid e.
What does the retriever learn? Since the knowledge re-
trieval of REALM is latent, it is not obvious how the train-
ing objective encourages meaningful retrievals. Here, we
show how it rewards retrievals that improve prediction ac-
curacy.
For a given query x and document z, recall that f (x, z) is
the “relevance score” that the knowledge retriever assigns
to document z. We can see how a single step of gradient
descent during REALM pre-training alters this score by an-
alyzing the gradient with respect to the parameters of the
knowledge retriever, θ:
∇ log p(y |x) =
∑
z∈Z

For each document z, the gradient encourages the retriever
to change the score f (x, z) by r(z) — increasing if r(z)
is positive, and decreasing if negative. The multiplier r(z)
is positive if and only if p(y |z, x) > p (y |x). The term
p(y |z, x) is the probability of predicting the correct output
y when using document z. The term p(y |x) is the expected
value of p(y |x, z) when randomly sampling a document
from p(z |x). Hence, document z receives a positive up-
date whenever it performs better than expected.
3This works because pre-training already yields a good
Embeddoc function. However, it is possible that refreshing the in-
dex would further improve performance.
3.4. Injecting inductive biases into pre-training
In the process of developing REALM, we discovered sev-
eral additional strategies that further guide the model to-
wards meaningful retrievals, described below.
Salient span masking During REALM pre-training, we
want to focus on examples x that require world knowledge
to predict the masked tokens. As explained in Section
2,
some MLM spans only require local context. To focus on
problems that require world knowledge, we mask salient
spans such as “ United Kingdom ” or “ July 1969 ”. We
use a BERT-based tagger trained on CoNLL-2003 data
(
Sang & De Meulder , 2003) to identify named entities, and
a regular expression to identify dates. We select and mask
one of these salient spans within a sentence for the masked
language modeling task. We show that this signiﬁcantly
outperforms other masking strategies in Section
4.5.
Null document Even with salient span masking, not all
masked tokens require world knowledge to predict. We
model this by adding an empty null document ∅ to the top
k retrieved documents, allowing appropriate credit to be as-
signed to a consistent sink when no retrieval is necessary.
Prohibiting trivial retrievals If the pre-training corpus
X and the knowledge corpus Z are the same, there exists
a trivial retrieval candidate z that is too informative: if the
masked sentence x comes from document z, the knowledge
augmented encoder can trivially predict y by looking at the
unmasked version of x in z. This results in a large positive
gradient for p(z |x). If this occurs too often, the knowledge
retriever ends up learning to look for exact string matches
between x and z, which does not capture other forms of
relevance.

Encoder or Retriever We ﬁrst aim to determine whether
REALM pre-training improves the retriever or the encoder,
or both. To do so, we can reset the parameters of either
the retriever or the encoder to their baseline state before
REALM pre-training, and feed that into ﬁne-tuning. Reset-
ting both the retriever and encoder reduces the system to
our main baseline, ORQA. We ﬁnd that both the encoder
and retriever beneﬁt from REALM training separately, but
the best result requires both components acting in unison.
Masking scheme We compare our salient span masking
scheme (Section
3.4) with (1) random token masking in-
troduced in BERT ( Devlin et al., 2018) and (2) random
span masking proposed by SpanBERT ( Joshi et al. , 2019).
While such salient span masking has not been shown to
be impactful in previous work with standard BERT train-
ing (
Joshi et al. , 2019), it is crucial for REALM. Intuitively,
the latent variable learning relies heavily on the utility of re-
trieval and is therefore more sensitive to a consistent lear n-
ing signal.
MIPS index refresh rate During pre-training, we run a
parallel process to re-embed corpus documents and rebuild
the MIPS index. This results in one index refresh per ap-
proximately 500 training steps. To demonstrate the impor-
tance of frequent index refreshes, we compare against using
a slower refresh rate. The results in Table
2 suggests that
a stale index can hurt model training, and further reducing
this staleness could offer better optimization.
Examples of retrieved documents Table
3 shows an
example of the REALM masked language model predic-
tion. In this example, “ Fermat” is the correct word, and
REALM (row (c)) gives the word a much high probability
compared to the BERT model (row (a)). Since REALM
manages to retrieve some documents with a related fact
(row (b)), the marginalized probability of the correct an-
swer dramatically increases. This shows that REALM is
able to retrieve document to ﬁll in the masked word even
though it is trained with unsupervised text only.
5. Discussion and Related Work
We previously discussed related methods for Open-QA.
Here we present several alternate ways of viewing REALM
that connect it to a broader set of ideas beyond Open-QA:
Language modeling with corpus as context Language
representation models have been incorporating contexts of
increasingly large scope when making predictions. Ex-
amples of this progression include models that condi-
tion on surrounding words (
Mikolov et al., 2013a;b), sen-
tences ( Kiros et al., 2015; Peters et al., 2018), and para-
graphs ( Radford et al., 2018; Devlin et al., 2018). We can
view REALM as a generalization of the above work to the
next level of scope: the entire text corpus.
Retrieve-and-edit with learned retrieval In order to
better explain the variance in the input text and en-
able controllable generation,
Guu et al. (2018) proposed
a language model with the retrieve-and-edit frame-
work (Hashimoto et al., 2018) that conditions on text with
high lexical overlap. REALM has a similar approach, ex-
cept that the model learns for itself which texts are most
useful for reducing perplexity.

Scalable grounded neural memory The document in-
dex can be viewed as a memory where the keys are
the document embeddings. From this view, our work
share motivations with works such as product key mem-
ory (
Lample et al., 2019), which enables sub-linear mem-
ory access in a memory network ( Weston et al., 2014;
Graves et al., 2014; Sukhbaatar et al., 2015), allowing
these scalable memory layers to be integrated into large
language models. One main difference is that our memo-
ries are grounded—each memory is associated with a docu-
ment rather than unnamed value vectors. This level of inter-
pretability is crucial for applications like Open-QA, wher e
users would require provenance for a predicted answer to
be trustworthy.

C. Adapting to new knowledge
An explicit retrieval system allows us to adapt to new
world knowledge simply by modifying the corpus docu-
ments. To demonstrate this ability, we replace the knowl-
edge corpus with a more recent version of Wikipedia cor-
pus after pre-training is done. When the input query is

coder will still end up remembering some world knowl-

the frequent mention of her name in Wikipedia articles.)

The null document ∅ described in Section

we deﬁne the retrieval utility (RU) of z for the masked
input x as the difference between the log-likelihood of
the knowledge-augmented encoder when conditioning on
z versus on ∅:
RU(z |x) = log p(y |z, x) − log p(y |∅, x). (2)
A negative RU shows that z is less useful for predicting y
than the null document. This could mean that z is irrelevant

not require world knowledge to predict, or that the world
knowledge is sufﬁciently commonplace it has been baked
into the model’s parameters. In practice, we ﬁnd that RU
increases steadily over the course of pre-training, and is
more predictive of good performance on the downstream
task of Open-QA than even the overall log-likelihood. An
example of how RU behaves over time and across different
settings is in Figure
4.

From this, we see that gradient descent on the REALM ob-
jective is equivalent to gradient descent on log p (z∗ |x).
This is none other than the typical maximum likelihood
training objective used in supervised learning, where z∗ is
the “gold” document.
C. Adapting to new knowledge
An explicit retrieval system allows us to adapt to new
world knowledge simply by modifying the corpus docu-
ments. To demonstrate this ability, we replace the knowl-
edge corpus with a more recent version of Wikipedia cor-
pus after pre-training is done. When the input query is
about a fact where the two corpora disagree, REALM can
change the prediction to reﬂect the updated information,
as exempliﬁed in Table
4. However, even with an ex-
plicit retrieval mechanism, the knowledge-augmented en-
coder will still end up remembering some world knowl-
edge, making the prediction of some input sentences not
updated with the new corpus. (For instance, the model pre-
dicts “ Thatcher” for “
is the prime minister
of United Kingdom. ” on both corpora, perhaps due to
the frequent mention of her name in Wikipedia articles.)
D. Retrieval Utility
The null document ∅ described in Section
3.4 provides a
way to measure the importance of a retrieved document z:
we deﬁne the retrieval utility (RU) of z for the masked
input x as the difference between the log-likelihood of
the knowledge-augmented encoder when conditioning on
z versus on ∅:
RU(z |x) = log p(y |z, x) − log p(y |∅, x). (2)
A negative RU shows that z is less useful for predicting y
than the null document. This could mean that z is irrelevant
to x, but could also mean that the masked tokens in x do
not require world knowledge to predict, or that the world
knowledge is sufﬁciently commonplace it has been baked
into the model’s parameters. In practice, we ﬁnd that RU
increases steadily over the course of pre-training, and is
more predictive of good performance on the downstream
task of Open-QA than even the overall log-likelihood. An
example of how RU behaves over time and across different
settings is in Figure
4.

3.4. Injecting inductive biases into pre-training
In the process of developing REALM, we discovered sev-
eral additional strategies that further guide the model to-
wards meaningful retrievals, described below.
Salient span masking During REALM pre-training, we
want to focus on examples x that require world knowledge
to predict the masked tokens. As explained in Section
2,
some MLM spans only require local context. To focus on
problems that require world knowledge, we mask salient
spans such as “ United Kingdom ” or “ July 1969 ”. We
use a BERT-based tagger trained on CoNLL-2003 data
(
Sang & De Meulder , 2003) to identify named entities, and
a regular expression to identify dates. We select and mask
one of these salient spans within a sentence for the masked
language modeling task. We show that this signiﬁcantly
outperforms other masking strategies in Section
4.5.
Null document Even with salient span masking, not all
masked tokens require world knowledge to predict. We
model this by adding an empty null document ∅ to the top
k retrieved documents, allowing appropriate credit to be as-
signed to a consistent sink when no retrieval is necessary.
Prohibiting trivial retrievals If the pre-training corpus
X and the knowledge corpus Z are the same, there exists
a trivial retrieval candidate z that is too informative: if the
masked sentence x comes from document z, the knowledge
augmented encoder can trivially predict y by looking at the
unmasked version of x in z. This results in a large positive
gradient for p(z |x). If this occurs too often, the knowledge
retriever ends up learning to look for exact string matches
between x and z, which does not capture other forms of
relevance.

els comprehend a single document, Open-QA models must
retain knowledge from millions of documents, since a ques-
tion could be about any of them.
We focus on Open-QA systems that utilize a textual knowl-
edge corpus Z as the knowledge source. Many of these
systems employ a retrieval-based approach: given a ques-
tion x, retrieve potentially relevant documents z from
the corpus Z, and then extract an answer y from the
documents (
Brill et al., 2002; Chen et al. , 2017; Lee et al. ,
2019). Our approach, REALM, is inspired by this
paradigm and extends it to language model pre-training.
Alternatively, some recent work has proposed generation-
based systems that apply a sequence-to-sequence model on
x to directly generate y token-by-token (Lewis et al., 2019;
Raffel et al., 2019). We will compare against state-of-the-
art systems from both paradigms in our experiments.
3. Approach
We start by formalizing REALM’s pre-training and ﬁne-
tuning tasks as a retrieve-then-predict generative process
in Section
3.1. Then in Section 3.2, we describe the model
architectures for each component of that process. In Sec-
tion
3.3, we show how to implement REALM pre-training
and ﬁne-tuning by maximizing the likelihood of REALM’s
generative process. En route, we address important compu-
tational challenges, explain why training works, and also
discuss strategies for injecting useful inductive biases. The
overall framework is illustrated in Figure
2.
3.1. REALM’s generative process
For both pre-training and ﬁne-tuning, REALM takes some
input x and learns a distribution p(y |x) over possible out-
puts y. For pre-training, the task is masked language mod-
eling: x is a sentence from a pre-training corpus X with
some tokens masked out, and the model must predict the
value of those missing tokens, y. For ﬁne-tuning, the task
is Open-QA: x is a question, and y is the answer.
REALM decomposes p(y |x) into two steps: retrieve, then
predict. Given an input x, we ﬁrst retrieve possibly helpful
documents z from a knowledge corpus Z. We model this as
a sample from the distribution p(z |x). Then, we condition
on both the retrieved z and the original input x to generate
the output y—modeled as p(y |z, x). To obtain the overall
likelihood of generating y, we treat z as a latent variable
and marginalize over all possible documents z, yielding
p(y |x) =

We now describe the two key components: the
neural knowledge retriever , which models p(z |x),
and the knowledge-augmented encoder , which models
p(y |z, x).

dense inner product model:

that map x and z respectively to d-dimensional vectors.
The relevance score f (x, z) between x and z is deﬁned as
the inner product of the vector embeddings. The retrieval

Encoder or Retriever We ﬁrst aim to determine whether
REALM pre-training improves the retriever or the encoder,
or both. To do so, we can reset the parameters of either
the retriever or the encoder to their baseline state before

ting both the retriever and encoder reduces the system to
our main baseline, ORQA. We ﬁnd that both the encoder
and retriever beneﬁt from REALM training separately, but
the best result requires both components acting in unison.
Masking scheme We compare our salient span masking
scheme (Section
3.4) with (1) random token masking in-
troduced in BERT ( Devlin et al., 2018) and (2) random
span masking proposed by SpanBERT ( Joshi et al. , 2019).
While such salient span masking has not been shown to
be impactful in previous work with standard BERT train-
ing (
Joshi et al. , 2019), it is crucial for REALM. Intuitively,
the latent variable learning relies heavily on the utility of re-
trieval and is therefore more sensitive to a consistent lear n-
ing signal.
MIPS index refresh rate During pre-training, we run a
parallel process to re-embed corpus documents and rebuild
the MIPS index. This results in one index refresh per ap-
proximately 500 training steps. To demonstrate the impor-
tance of frequent index refreshes, we compare against using
a slower refresh rate. The results in Table

a stale index can hurt model training, and further reducing

5. Discussion and Related Work
We previously discussed related methods for Open-QA.
Here we present several alternate ways of viewing REALM
that connect it to a broader set of ideas beyond Open-QA:
Language modeling with corpus as context Language
representation models have been incorporating contexts of
increasingly large scope when making predictions. Ex-
amples of this progression include models that condi-
tion on surrounding words (
Mikolov et al., 2013a;b), sen-
tences ( Kiros et al., 2015; Peters et al., 2018), and para-
graphs ( Radford et al., 2018; Devlin et al., 2018). We can
view REALM as a generalization of the above work to the
next level of scope: the entire text corpus.
Retrieve-and-edit with learned retrieval In order to
better explain the variance in the input text and en-
able controllable generation,
Guu et al. (2018) proposed
a language model with the retrieve-and-edit frame-
work (Hashimoto et al., 2018) that conditions on text with
high lexical overlap. REALM has a similar approach, ex-
cept that the model learns for itself which texts are most
useful for reducing perplexity.

questions.baseline_answer:
The REALM model uses a retrieve-then-predict generative framework for both pre-training (masked language modeling) and fine-tuning (Open-QA). The framework works as follows:

1. Given an input \(x\) (a sentence with masked tokens for pre-training or a question for fine-tuning), the model first retrieves one or more potentially relevant documents \(z\) from a large textual knowledge corpus \(Z\). The retrieval step is modeled as sampling \(z\) from a conditional distribution \(p(z|x)\).

2. Then, conditioned on both the input \(x\) and the retrieved document \(z\), the model generates the output \(y\) (masked token(s) or the answer), modeled as \(p(y|z, x)\).

3. To obtain the overall likelihood of generating \(y\) given \(x\), the model treats \(z\) as a latent variable and marginalizes over all \(z \in Z\), so:
   \[
   p(y|x) = \sum_{z \in Z} p(y|z, x) p(z|x).
   \]

Thus, the role of the latent document variable \(z\) is to represent the (potentially) relevant documents from the knowledge corpus that can aid in predicting \(y\) given \(x\). The model learns to assign higher probability to documents that improve prediction accuracy.

The retriever models \(p(z|x)\) using dense inner-product similarity between vector embeddings of the query \(x\) and documents \(z\):
\[
p(z|x) = \frac{\exp f(x,z)}{\sum_{z'} \exp f(x,z')}, \quad \text{where } f(x,z) = \text{Embed}_{input}(x)^\top \text{Embed}_{doc}(z).
\]
These embeddings are produced by BERT-style Transformers, and the similarity score \(f(x,z)\) measures relevance of document \(z\) to input \(x\).

The knowledge-augmented encoder models \(p(y|z,x)\) and conditions the prediction on both the input and the retrieved document.

The training objective maximizes the marginal likelihood \(p(y|x)\) by jointly optimizing the retriever and the encoder. Gradient updates to the retriever's scoring function encourage increasing scores for documents \(z\) where the conditional probability \(p(y|z,x)\) exceeds the marginal \(p(y|x)\),
questions.orchestrator_answer:
REALM’s retrieve-then-predict framework works by incorporating a learned textual knowledge retriever that queries external documents during language model pre-training [E1]. Unlike traditional models that embed knowledge solely within their parameters, REALM explicitly exposes world knowledge by retrieving relevant textual information through this retriever component [E2]. This approach augments the model’s internal understanding by allowing it to access external textual information dynamically, rather than relying only on parameterized knowledge [E3]. Consequently, REALM enables the use of external knowledge sources as part of the prediction process instead of depending exclusively on stored knowledge within the model’s parameters [E4][E5].  

The evidence notes emphasize the role of a learned retriever and the explicit use of external knowledge but do not provide direct details on the latent document variable z or explicitly describe it as representing the retrieved text used before prediction. Therefore, the specifics regarding the latent variable z and its operational role in REALM’s framework are not covered by the provided evidence.

is positive if and only if p(y |z, x) > p (y |x). The term
p(y |z, x) is the probability of predicting the correct output
y when using document z. The term p(y |x) is the expected
value of p(y |x, z) when randomly sampling a document
from p(z |x). Hence, document z receives a positive up-
date whenever it performs better than expected.
3This works because pre-training already yields a good
Embeddoc function. However, it is possible that refreshing the in-
dex would further improve performance.
3.4. Injecting inductive biases into pre-training
In the process of developing REALM, we discovered sev-
eral additional strategies that further guide the model to-
wards meaningful retrievals, described below.
Salient span masking During REALM pre-training, we
want to focus on examples x that require world knowledge
to predict the masked tokens. As explained in Section
2,
some MLM spans only require local context. To focus on
problems that require world knowledge, we mask salient
spans such as “ United Kingdom ” or “ July 1969 ”. We
use a BERT-based tagger trained on CoNLL-2003 data
(
Sang & De Meulder , 2003) to identify named entities, and
a regular expression to identify dates. We select and mask
one of these salient spans within a sentence for the masked
language modeling task. We show that this signiﬁcantly
outperforms other masking strategies in Section
4.5.
Null document Even with salient span masking, not all
masked tokens require world knowledge to predict. We
model this by adding an empty null document ∅ to the top
k retrieved documents, allowing appropriate credit to be as-
signed to a consistent sink when no retrieval is necessary.
Prohibiting trivial retrievals If the pre-training corpus
X and the knowledge corpus Z are the same, there exists
a trivial retrieval candidate z that is too informative: if the
masked sentence x comes from document z, the knowledge
augmented encoder can trivially predict y by looking at the
unmasked version of x in z. This results in a large positive
gradient for p(z |x). If this occurs too often, the knowledge
retriever ends up learning to look for exact string matches
between x and z, which does not capture other forms of
relevance.

questions.baseline_answer:
The paper trains the retriever when the retrieved document is latent by using a training objective that rewards retrievals improving prediction accuracy. Specifically, for a given query \( x \) and document \( z \), the model assigns a relevance score \( f(x, z) \) through the knowledge retriever. The gradient of the log-likelihood with respect to the retriever parameters \( \theta \) is given by:

\[
\nabla \log p(y|x) = \sum_{z \in Z} r(z) \nabla f(x, z)
\]

where

\[
r(z) = \left[\frac{p(y|z,x)}{p(y|x)} - 1\right] p(z|x)
\]

Here, \( p(y|z,x) \) is the probability of correctly predicting the output \( y \) when conditioning on document \( z \), and \( p(y|x) \) is the expected probability over all documents. This mechanism encourages the retriever to increase the score of documents that lead to better-than-expected predictions (positive \( r(z) \)) and decrease the scores of less helpful documents.

Thus, even though retrieval is latent (documents are not explicitly labeled for retrieval), the retriever learns by receiving gradient signals that flow back from the prediction loss through the relevance scores. This enables the retriever to self-improve by favoring documents that enhance overall prediction accuracy.

Additional training strategies include:
- Masking salient spans (e.g., named entities or dates) that typically require external knowledge, focusing learning on relevant retrieval.
- Including a null document to handle cases where no retrieval is necessary.
- Avoiding trivial retrievals (i.e., exact matches from the same document) to prevent overfitting on superficial lexical overlap.

These details collectively explain how the latent retriever is trained and how the learning signal flows back into retrieval during model training [3].

Before making each prediction, the language model uses
the retriever to retrieve documents

to help inform its prediction. Learning this model end-to-

The key intuition of REALM is to train the retriever us-
ing a performance-based signal from unsupervised text:
a retrieval that improves the language model’s perplex-
ity is helpful and should be rewarded, while an un-
informative retrieval should be penalized. For exam-
ple, in Figure
1, if the model needs to ﬁll the blank
in “ the at the top of the pyramid ”, the re-
triever should be rewarded for selecting a document con-
taining “The pyramidion on top allows for less
material higher up the pyramid ”. We achieve this
behavior by modeling our retrieve-then-predict approach
as a latent variable language model and optimizing the
marginal likelihood.
Incorporating a large-scale neural retrieval module durin g
pre-training constitutes a signiﬁcant computational chal -
lenge, since the retriever must consider millions of candi-
date documents for each pre-training step, and we must
backpropagate through its decisions. To address this, we
structure the retriever such that the computation performe d
for each document can be cached and asynchronously up-
dated, and selection of the best documents can be formu-
lated as Maximum Inner Product Search (MIPS).
Numerous prior works have demonstrated the bene-
ﬁt of adding a discrete retrieval step to neural net-
works (
Miller et al. , 2016; Chen et al. , 2017), but did not
apply the framework to language model pre-training and
employed non-learned retrievers to handle large-scale doc -
ument collections. In the language modeling literature, th e
k-Nearest Neighbor Language Model (
Khandelwal et al.,

text, not a labeled example.

questions.baseline_answer:
REALM employs several approximations and engineering choices to make retrieval over a large corpus practical during training and inference:

1. **Latent Variable Language Model and Marginal Likelihood Optimization:** REALM models the retrieve-then-predict approach as a latent variable language model and optimizes the marginal likelihood. This formulation allows learning the retriever using performance-based signals without requiring explicit supervision for retrieved documents [2].

2. **Caching and Asynchronous Updates:** To handle the computational challenge of considering millions of candidate documents during pre-training, REALM structures the retriever such that computations performed for each document can be cached and asynchronously updated. This reduces the computational load for repeated access to document embeddings [2].

3. **Maximum Inner Product Search (MIPS):** Selection of the best documents is formulated as a Maximum Inner Product Search, which is an efficient approximate nearest neighbor method allowing fast retrieval in high-dimensional vector spaces [2].

4. **Limited Number of Retrieved Documents:** For each example, REALM retrieves and marginalizes over only 8 candidate documents (including a null document). This reduces the number of documents considered per query, balancing performance and efficiency. Compared to other retrievers that often retrieve 20 to 80 documents, this small candidate set significantly speeds up retrieval [1].

5. **Parallel Document Embedding:** The document embedding step for the MIPS index is parallelized over multiple TPUs (e.g., 16 TPUs), which speeds up the computation of embeddings needed for retrieval [1].

6. **Single Machine Feasibility:** Despite the large corpus, the entire model (including the retrieval) can be run on a single machine with a 12GB GPU thanks to these efficiencies and approximations [1].

These design and engineering trade-offs allow REALM to efficiently integrate retrieval during pre-training and inference over large textual corpora.

Encoder or Retriever We ﬁrst aim to determine whether
REALM pre-training improves the retriever or the encoder,
or both. To do so, we can reset the parameters of either
the retriever or the encoder to their baseline state before
REALM pre-training, and feed that into ﬁne-tuning. Reset-
ting both the retriever and encoder reduces the system to
our main baseline, ORQA. We ﬁnd that both the encoder
and retriever beneﬁt from REALM training separately, but
the best result requires both components acting in unison.
Masking scheme We compare our salient span masking
scheme (Section
3.4) with (1) random token masking in-
troduced in BERT ( Devlin et al., 2018) and (2) random
span masking proposed by SpanBERT ( Joshi et al. , 2019).
While such salient span masking has not been shown to
be impactful in previous work with standard BERT train-
ing (
Joshi et al. , 2019), it is crucial for REALM. Intuitively,
the latent variable learning relies heavily on the utility of re-
trieval and is therefore more sensitive to a consistent lear n-
ing signal.
MIPS index refresh rate During pre-training, we run a
parallel process to re-embed corpus documents and rebuild
the MIPS index. This results in one index refresh per ap-
proximately 500 training steps. To demonstrate the impor-
tance of frequent index refreshes, we compare against using
a slower refresh rate. The results in Table
2 suggests that
a stale index can hurt model training, and further reducing
this staleness could offer better optimization.
Examples of retrieved documents Table
3 shows an
example of the REALM masked language model predic-
tion. In this example, “ Fermat” is the correct word, and
REALM (row (c)) gives the word a much high probability
compared to the BERT model (row (a)). Since REALM
manages to retrieve some documents with a related fact
(row (b)), the marginalized probability of the correct an-
swer dramatically increases. This shows that REALM is
able to retrieve document to ﬁll in the masked word even
though it is trained with unsupervised text only.
5. Discussion and Related Work
We previously discussed related methods for Open-QA.
Here we present several alternate ways of viewing REALM
that connect it to a broader set of ideas beyond Open-QA:
Language modeling with corpus as context Language
representation models have been incorporating contexts of
increasingly large scope when making predictions. Ex-
amples of this progression include models that condi-
tion on surrounding words (
Mikolov et al., 2013a;b), sen-
tences ( Kiros et al., 2015; Peters et al., 2018), and para-
graphs ( Radford et al., 2018; Devlin et al., 2018). We can
view REALM as a generalization of the above work to the
next level of scope: the entire text corpus.
Retrieve-and-edit with learned retrieval In order to
better explain the variance in the input text and en-
able controllable generation,
Guu et al. (2018) proposed
a language model with the retrieve-and-edit frame-
work (Hashimoto et al., 2018) that conditions on text with
high lexical overlap. REALM has a similar approach, ex-
cept that the model learns for itself which texts are most
useful for reducing perplexity.

Table 1 shows the accuracy of different approaches on the
three Open-QA datasets. REALM outperform all previous
approaches by a signiﬁcant margin. Table
1 also shows the
number of parameters for each model.
As reported in the concurrent work of Roberts et al. (2020),
the generative Open-QA systems based on T5 are surpris-
ingly powerful, with the largest T5-11B model outperform-
ing the previous best Open-QA system. Increasing the size
of T5 yields consistent improvement, but comes at signif-
icant computational cost (from Base to 11B, the model is
50 times larger, and gains roughly 5 points in accuracy). In
contrast, REALM outperforms the largest T5-11B model
while being 30 times smaller. It is also important to note
that T5 accesses additional reading comprehension data
from SQuAD during its pre-training (100,000+ examples).
Access to such data could also beneﬁt REALM, but was not
used in our experiments.
Among all systems, the most direct comparison with

Lee et al., 2019), where the ﬁne-tuning

improvement of REALM over ORQA is purely due to bet-

questions.baseline_answer:
REALM's retrieval-based approach differs from storing knowledge only in model parameters by explicitly exposing the role of world knowledge through the use of a learned textual knowledge retriever. This makes the knowledge storage more interpretable and modular because the knowledge is accessed directly from an external knowledge base rather than being implicitly embedded within the model's parameters. In contrast, traditional models that store knowledge solely in their parameters do not provide this explicit retrieval mechanism, making it harder to interpret and update the knowledge the model relies on. Thus, REALM augments language model pre-training with retrieval, offering a clearer and more modular way to handle knowledge compared to relying exclusively on parameter storage [1][2][3][4][5].

decide what knowledge to retrieve and use during inference.
Before making each prediction, the language model uses
the retriever to retrieve documents
1 from a large corpus
such as Wikipedia, and then attends over those documents
to help inform its prediction. Learning this model end-to-
end requires backpropagating through a retrieval step that
considers an entire corpus of textual knowledge, as shown
in Figure
1.
The key intuition of REALM is to train the retriever us-
ing a performance-based signal from unsupervised text:
a retrieval that improves the language model’s perplex-
ity is helpful and should be rewarded, while an un-
informative retrieval should be penalized. For exam-
ple, in Figure
1, if the model needs to ﬁll the blank
in “ the at the top of the pyramid ”, the re-
triever should be rewarded for selecting a document con-
taining “The pyramidion on top allows for less
material higher up the pyramid ”. We achieve this
behavior by modeling our retrieve-then-predict approach
as a latent variable language model and optimizing the
marginal likelihood.
Incorporating a large-scale neural retrieval module durin g
pre-training constitutes a signiﬁcant computational chal -
lenge, since the retriever must consider millions of candi-
date documents for each pre-training step, and we must
backpropagate through its decisions. To address this, we
structure the retriever such that the computation performe d
for each document can be cached and asynchronously up-
dated, and selection of the best documents can be formu-
lated as Maximum Inner Product Search (MIPS).
Numerous prior works have demonstrated the bene-
ﬁt of adding a discrete retrieval step to neural net-
works (
Miller et al. , 2016; Chen et al. , 2017), but did not
apply the framework to language model pre-training and
employed non-learned retrievers to handle large-scale doc -
ument collections. In the language modeling literature, th e
k-Nearest Neighbor Language Model (
Khandelwal et al.,
2019) ( kNN-LM) retrieves similar LM examples to im-
prove memorization. However, kNN-LM was not ﬁne-
tuned for downstream tasks, perhaps because it is unclear
how to adapt the retrieval mechanism: a kNN can only use
examples labeled for the target task—during ﬁne-tuning,
this precludes LM examples, which contain the desired
world knowledge. In contrast, REALM’s retriever is de-
signed to transfer to other tasks, and the retrieval is just
text, not a labeled example.
We evaluate our approach by ﬁne-tuning the mod-
els pre-trained with REALM on the task of Open-
domain Question Answering (Open-QA), one of the most
knowledge-intensive tasks in natural language process-
ing. We evaluate on three popular Open-QA bench-
marks ( NATURAL QUESTIONS -O PEN , WEB QUESTIONS , and
CURATED TREC ) and compare to state-of-the-art Open-QA
models, including both extremely large models that store
knowledge implicitly (such as T5) as well as previous ap-
proaches that also use a knowledge retriever to access ex-
ternal knowledge, but implement retrieval in a more heuris-
tic fashion (
Lee et al. , 2019; Min et al. , 2019a; Asai et al. ,
2019). REALM achieves new state-of-the-art results on all
three benchmarks, signiﬁcantly outperforming all previou s
systems by 4-16% absolute accuracy. We also demonstrate
qualitative beneﬁts of REALM, including interpretability
and modularity.

guage, usually from unlabeled text corpora. The resulting
pre-trained model can then be further trained ( ﬁne-tuned)
for a downstream task of primary interest (in our case,
Open-QA), often leading to better generalization than trai n-
ing from scratch (

Before making each prediction, the language model uses
the retriever to retrieve documents

such as Wikipedia, and then attends over those documents
to help inform its prediction. Learning this model end-to-
end requires backpropagating through a retrieval step that
considers an entire corpus of textual knowledge, as shown
in Figure
1.
The key intuition of REALM is to train the retriever us-
ing a performance-based signal from unsupervised text:
a retrieval that improves the language model’s perplex-
ity is helpful and should be rewarded, while an un-
informative retrieval should be penalized. For exam-
ple, in Figure
1, if the model needs to ﬁll the blank
in “ the at the top of the pyramid ”, the re-
triever should be rewarded for selecting a document con-
taining “The pyramidion on top allows for less
material higher up the pyramid ”. We achieve this
behavior by modeling our retrieve-then-predict approach
as a latent variable language model and optimizing the
marginal likelihood.
Incorporating a large-scale neural retrieval module durin g
pre-training constitutes a signiﬁcant computational chal -
lenge, since the retriever must consider millions of candi-
date documents for each pre-training step, and we must
backpropagate through its decisions. To address this, we
structure the retriever such that the computation performe d
for each document can be cached and asynchronously up-
dated, and selection of the best documents can be formu-
lated as Maximum Inner Product Search (MIPS).
Numerous prior works have demonstrated the bene-
ﬁt of adding a discrete retrieval step to neural net-
works (
Miller et al. , 2016; Chen et al. , 2017), but did not
apply the framework to language model pre-training and
employed non-learned retrievers to handle large-scale doc -
ument collections. In the language modeling literature, th e
k-Nearest Neighbor Language Model (
Khandelwal et al.,
2019) ( kNN-LM) retrieves similar LM examples to im-
prove memorization. However, kNN-LM was not ﬁne-
tuned for downstream tasks, perhaps because it is unclear
how to adapt the retrieval mechanism: a kNN can only use
examples labeled for the target task—during ﬁne-tuning,
this precludes LM examples, which contain the desired
world knowledge. In contrast, REALM’s retriever is de-
signed to transfer to other tasks, and the retrieval is just
text, not a labeled example.

CURATED TREC ) and compare to state-of-the-art Open-QA

In the process of developing REALM, we discovered sev-
eral additional strategies that further guide the model to-
wards meaningful retrievals, described below.
Salient span masking During REALM pre-training, we
want to focus on examples x that require world knowledge
to predict the masked tokens. As explained in Section
2,
some MLM spans only require local context. To focus on
problems that require world knowledge, we mask salient
spans such as “ United Kingdom ” or “ July 1969 ”. We
use a BERT-based tagger trained on CoNLL-2003 data
(
Sang & De Meulder , 2003) to identify named entities, and
a regular expression to identify dates. We select and mask
one of these salient spans within a sentence for the masked
language modeling task. We show that this signiﬁcantly
outperforms other masking strategies in Section
4.5.
Null document Even with salient span masking, not all
masked tokens require world knowledge to predict. We
model this by adding an empty null document ∅ to the top
k retrieved documents, allowing appropriate credit to be as-
signed to a consistent sink when no retrieval is necessary.
Prohibiting trivial retrievals If the pre-training corpus
X and the knowledge corpus Z are the same, there exists
a trivial retrieval candidate z that is too informative: if the
masked sentence x comes from document z, the knowledge
augmented encoder can trivially predict y by looking at the
unmasked version of x in z. This results in a large positive
gradient for p(z |x). If this occurs too often, the knowledge
retriever ends up learning to look for exact string matches
between x and z, which does not capture other forms of
relevance.

questions.baseline_answer:
The ablation or diagnostic evidence showing that retrieval pre-training improves REALM beyond just the reader component is that the authors measure how often the gold answer appears in the top-5 retrievals before any fine-tuning is applied. This metric isolates the contribution of improving the retriever during pre-training, independent of the reader fine-tuning. Reporting this metric alongside the end-to-end results demonstrates that the improvements come from a better retriever component, not solely from the reader model [1], [2], [4], [5].

questions.orchestrator_answer:
The available evidence notes consistently describe that REALM (Retrieval-Augmented Language Model) pre-training enhances language model pre-training by integrating a learned textual knowledge retriever, which explicitly exposes the model to external world knowledge rather than storing all knowledge solely within its parameters [E1][E2]. Unlike models that embed knowledge only in their parameters, REALM’s approach explicitly incorporates world knowledge by querying the model with retrieved textual information [E3][E4][E5].

However, none of the provided extracts include explicit ablation studies or diagnostic experiments that separately measure the impact of retrieval pre-training as distinct from improvements attributable only to the reader component [E1][E2][E3][E4][E5]. Additionally, the notes lack specific numerical results or controlled comparisons that isolate the contribution of retrieval pre-training to REALM’s performance gains [E1][E2][E3][E4][E5].

Therefore, based on the available evidence, there is no direct ablation or diagnostic evidence presented that conclusively demonstrates that retrieval pre-training, rather than solely the reader component, is responsible for the observed improvements in REALM [E1][E2][E3][E4][E5].

questions.baseline_answer:
REALM improves open-domain question answering (QA) performance through several interconnected factors involving retrieval quality, pre-training, and downstream fine-tuning.

1. **Retrieval Quality Improvement through Pre-Training**  
Before any fine-tuning on downstream tasks, REALM demonstrates improved retrieval quality by increasing how often the gold answer appears among the top-5 retrieved documents. This metric more directly isolates the contribution of enhancing the retriever during pre-training. The better the retriever at this stage, the more likely the model will locate relevant evidence to answer questions accurately [1][2][3][4][5].

2. **End-to-End Improvements from Component Ablation Studies**  
Studies that ablate (remove) critical components of REALM reveal the contribution each part makes to overall QA performance. The ablation results, especially on the NaturalQuestions-Open dataset, show that both retrieval and reading components are crucial. This implies that the improvements in retrieval quality during pre-training translate into better downstream QA outcomes once fine-tuning is applied, highlighting the synergy between pre-training the retriever and fine-tuning the entire system end-to-end [1][2][3][4][5].

3. **Downstream Fine-Tuning Amplifies Pre-Trained Retrieval Gains**  
Although retrieval quality improvements are clear prior to fine-tuning, applying fine-tuning further boosts the model’s ability to answer questions by leveraging the improved retriever and reader jointly. This combination maximizes gains in open-domain QA performance, with REALM benefiting from the retrieval improvements established during pre-training to inform better downstream reading comprehension [1][2][3][4][5].

In summary, REALM's enhanced open-domain QA performance is grounded in its effective retriever pre-training, which increases the presence of gold answers in top retrievals, and this foundational improvement is amplified by downstream fine-tuning that integrates retrieval and reading modules cohesively.

Citations:  
[1][2][3][4][5] — data/2002.08909v1.pdf, chunks 2444.0 to 2448.0
questions.orchestrator_answer: REALM improves open-domain question answering (QA) performance in part by enhancing the retrieval quality of its retriever component through pre-training, as evidenced by analyses on the NaturalQuestions-Open dataset after ablating critical parts of the model [E1][E2][E3][E4][E5]. Multiple studies consistently report how often the gold answer appears in the top-5 retrieved documents before any fine-tuning is applied, using this metric to evaluate the retriever’s performance independently of downstream fine-tuning [E1][E2][E3][E4][E5]. This frequency metric isolates the contribution of retrieval improvements during pre-training, distinguishing it from end-to-end QA performance that includes fine-tuning effects [E1][E2][E3][E4][E5]. Specifically, the presence of the gold answer in the top-5 retrievals prior to fine-tuning serves as a key indicator of retrieval quality, demonstrating that pre-training significantly improves the retriever’s ability to surface relevant documents for answering questions [E1][E2]. However, the evidence does not explicitly quantify the extent to which retrieval improvements versus downstream fine-tuning each contribute to overall QA gains, so the precise balance between these factors remains uncertain based on the provided notes.

We previously discussed related methods for Open-QA.
Here we present several alternate ways of viewing REALM
that connect it to a broader set of ideas beyond Open-QA:
Language modeling with corpus as context Language

increasingly large scope when making predictions. Ex-

next level of scope: the entire text corpus.

better explain the variance in the input text and en-

a language model with the retrieve-and-edit frame-

useful for reducing perplexity.

ple, in Figure

as a latent variable language model and optimizing the

Incorporating a large-scale neural retrieval module durin g
pre-training constitutes a signiﬁcant computational chal -
lenge, since the retriever must consider millions of candi-

structure the retriever such that the computation performe d
for each document can be cached and asynchronously up-

Numerous prior works have demonstrated the bene-

works (
Miller et al. , 2016; Chen et al. , 2017), but did not
apply the framework to language model pre-training and
employed non-learned retrievers to handle large-scale doc -
ument collections. In the language modeling literature, th e
k-Nearest Neighbor Language Model (
Khandelwal et al.,
2019) ( kNN-LM) retrieves similar LM examples to im-

domain Question Answering (Open-QA), one of the most
knowledge-intensive tasks in natural language process-

CURATED TREC ) and compare to state-of-the-art Open-QA
models, including both extremely large models that store
knowledge implicitly (such as T5) as well as previous ap-
proaches that also use a knowledge retriever to access ex-
ternal knowledge, but implement retrieval in a more heuris-
tic fashion (
Lee et al. , 2019; Min et al. , 2019a; Asai et al. ,
2019). REALM achieves new state-of-the-art results on all
three benchmarks, signiﬁcantly outperforming all previou s

guage, usually from unlabeled text corpora. The resulting
pre-trained model can then be further trained ( ﬁne-tuned)
for a downstream task of primary interest (in our case,
Open-QA), often leading to better generalization than trai n-
ing from scratch (
Dai & Le, 2015; Radford et al.

Language model pre-training The goal of language
model pre-training is to learn useful representations of la n-
guage, usually from unlabeled text corpora. The resulting
pre-trained model can then be further trained ( ﬁne-tuned)
for a downstream task of primary interest (in our case,
Open-QA), often leading to better generalization than trai n-
ing from scratch (
Dai & Le, 2015; Radford et al. , 2019).
We focus on the masked language model 2 (MLM) variant
of pre-training popularized by BERT ( Devlin et al., 2018).
In its basic form, an MLM is trained to predict the miss-
ing tokens in an input text passage. Given an unlabeled
pre-training corpus X (e.g., Wikipedia text), a training ex-
ample (x, y) can be generated by randomly masking to-
kens in a sampled piece of text (e.g., x = “The [MASK]
is the currency [MASK] the UK ”; y = (“pound”,
“of”)). The model uses its representation of the masked
input x to predict the token that should go in each mask.
A good MLM must learn to encode syntactic and semantic
information (e.g., to predict “ of”) as well as some world
knowledge (e.g., to predict “ pound”).
Open-domain question answering (Open-QA) To mea-
sure a model’s ability to incorporate world knowledge, we
need a downstream task where world knowledge is criti-
cal. Perhaps one of the most knowledge-intensive tasks in
natural language processing is open-domain question an-
swering (Open-QA): given a question x such as “ What is
the currency of the UK? ”, a model must output the
correct answer string y, “pound”. The “open” part of Open-
QA refers to the fact that the model does not receive a pre-
identiﬁed document that is known to contain the answer,
unlike traditional reading comprehension (RC) tasks such
as SQuAD (
Rajpurkar et al., 2016; 2018). While RC mod-
1We use the term “document” loosely to refer to a passage
from the knowledge corpus, not necessarily a whole article.
2Strictly speaking, MLM is not a standard language model,
since it does not deﬁne a distribution over the entire sequen ce
of tokens. In the paper we sometimes abuse the term “language
model” slightly to make the phrase shorter.

4. Experiments
We now evaluate our approach on the Open-QA task. In
this section, we describe in detail the benchmarks used and
the different approaches to which we compare empirically.
4.1. Open-QA Benchmarks
A number of benchmarks have been proposed for Open-
QA. In this work, we focus on datasets where the ques-
tion writers did not already know the answer. This yields
questions that reﬂect more realistic information-seeking
needs, and also avoids artifacts that can arise if the ques-
tion is formulated with a particular answer in mind. A
deeper justiﬁcation is given in
Lee et al. (2019). In all
cases, the predicted answer is evaluated via exact match
with any reference answer, following previous Open-QA
work (
Chen et al., 2017).
NaturalQuestions-Open The NaturalQuestions dataset
(Kwiatkowski et al., 2019) consists of naturally occurring
Google queries and their answers. Each answer also comes
with an “answer type”: following Lee et al. (2019), we only
keep questions that are categorized as “short answer type”
with at most ﬁve tokens. The dataset also provides a sug-
gested Wikipedia document to retrieve; like all models we
compare against, we do not provide this to our model.
WebQuestions The WebQuestions dataset (
Berant et al. ,
2013) was collected from the Google Suggest API, using
one seed question and expanding the set to related ques-
tions. We follow the setting deﬁned by
Chen et al. (2017).
CuratedTrec The CuratedTrec dataset is a collection of
question-answer pairs drawn from real user queries issued
on sites such as MSNSearch and AskJeeves. To account for
multiple correct answers or different spelling variations, the
answers in this dataset are deﬁned as regular expressions
that match all correct answers. It is unclear how to train
generation-based models with this type of supervision, so
we do not evaluate them on this dataset.
4.2. Approaches compared
Retrieval-based Open-QA Most existing Open-QA sys-
tems answer the input question by ﬁrst retrieving poten-
tially relevant documents from a knowledge corpus, and
then using a reading comprehension system to extract an
answer from the documents. In this paradigm, the knowl-
edge is stored explicitly in the corpus. We wish to compare
different methods for implementing retrieval.
Many approaches use non-learned heuristic retrieval such
as sparse bag-of-words matching (
Robertson et al. , 2009)
or entity linking on the question to select a small set of rel-
evant documents (e.g., 20). These documents are typically
then re-ranked using a learned model, but coverage may be
limited by the initial heuristic retrieval step. Approache s
such as DrQA (
Chen et al., 2017), HardEM ( Min et al. ,
2019a), GraphRetriever ( Min et al. , 2019b), and PathRe-
triever (Asai et al., 2019) in Table 1 are in this category.
Some recent approaches have proposed to implement learn-
able retrieval using a MIPS index. ORQA (
Lee et al. , 2019)
formulates Open-QA using a similar latent variable model
as REALM, and also trains by maximizing the marginal
likelihood. However, REALM adds a novel language
model pre-training step, and backpropagates into the MIPS
index, rather than using a ﬁxed index. In Table
1, we di-
rectly compare the two. It is also important to note that
the retrievers for both REALM pretraining and ORQA are
initialized using the Inverse Cloze Task, described in Sec-
tion
3.4.

approach to Open-QA is to model it as a sequence pre-
diction task: simply encode the question, and then decode
the answer token-by-token based on the encoding. While
it was initially unclear how large amounts of knowledge
could be injected into the model, GPT-2 (
Radford et al.,
2019) hinted at the possibility of directly generating an-
swers without using any given context via sequence-to-
sequence. However, their performance was not competi-
tive possibly due to the lack of ﬁne-tuning.

[Page 3]
REALM: Retrieval-Augmented Language Model Pre-Training
els comprehend a single document, Open-QA models must
retain knowledge from millions of documents, since a ques-
tion could be about any of them.
We focus on Open-QA systems that utilize a textual knowl-
edge corpus Z as the knowledge source. Many of these
systems employ a retrieval-based approach: given a ques-
tion x, retrieve potentially relevant documents z from
the corpus Z, and then extract an answer y from the
documents (
Brill et al., 2002; Chen et al. , 2017; Lee et al. ,
2019). Our approach, REALM, is inspired by this
paradigm and extends it to language model pre-training.
Alternatively, some recent work has proposed generation-
based systems that apply a sequence-to-sequence model on
x to directly generate y token-by-token (Lewis et al., 2019;
Raffel et al., 2019). We will compare against state-of-the-
art systems from both paradigms in our experiments.
3. Approach
We start by formalizing REALM’s pre-training and ﬁne-
tuning tasks as a retrieve-then-predict generative process
in Section
3.1. Then in Section 3.2, we describe the model
architectures for each component of that process. In Sec-
tion
3.3, we show how to implement REALM pre-training
and ﬁne-tuning by maximizing the likelihood of REALM’s
generative process. En route, we address important compu-
tational challenges, explain why training works, and also
discuss strategies for injecting useful inductive biases. The
overall framework is illustrated in Figure
2.
3.1. REALM’s generative process
For both pre-training and ﬁne-tuning, REALM takes some
input x and learns a distribution p(y |x) over possible out-
puts y. For pre-training, the task is masked language mod-
eling: x is a sentence from a pre-training corpus X with
some tokens masked out, and the model must predict the
value of those missing tokens, y. For ﬁne-tuning, the task
is Open-QA: x is a question, and y is the answer.
REALM decomposes p(y |x) into two steps: retrieve, then
predict. Given an input x, we ﬁrst retrieve possibly helpful
documents z from a knowledge corpus Z. We model this as
a sample from the distribution p(z |x). Then, we condition
on both the retrieved z and the original input x to generate
the output y—modeled as p(y |z, x). To obtain the overall
likelihood of generating y, we treat z as a latent variable
and marginalize over all possible documents z, yielding

that map x and z respectively to d-dimensional vectors.
The relevance score f (x, z) between x and z is deﬁned as
the inner product of the vector embeddings. The retrieval
distribution is the softmax over all relevance scores.
We implement the embedding functions using BERT-style
Transformers (
Devlin et al., 2018). Following standard
practices, we join spans of text by applying wordpiece tok-
enization, separating them with [SEP] tokens, preﬁxing a
[CLS] token, and appending a ﬁnal [SEP] token.

Abstract
Language model pre-training has been shown to
capture a surprising amount of world knowledge,
crucial for NLP tasks such as question answer-
ing. However, this knowledge is stored implic-
itly in the parameters of a neural network, requir-
ing ever-larger networks to cover more facts. To
capture knowledge in a more modular and inter-
pretable way, we augment language model pre-
training with a latent knowledge retriever, which
allows the model to retrieve and attend over doc-
uments from a large corpus such as Wikipedia,
used during pre-training, ﬁne-tuning and infer-
ence. For the ﬁrst time, we show how to pre-
train such a knowledge retriever in an unsuper-
vised manner, using masked language model-
ing as the learning signal and backpropagating
through a retrieval step that considers millions
of documents. We demonstrate the effective-
ness of Retrieval-Augmented Language Model
pre-training (REALM) by ﬁne-tuning on the chal-
lenging task of Open-domain Question Answer-
ing (Open-QA). We compare against state-of-the-
art models for both explicit and implicit knowl-
edge storage on three popular Open-QA bench-
marks, and ﬁnd that we outperform all previous
methods by a signiﬁcant margin (4-16% absolute
accuracy), while also providing qualitative bene-
ﬁts such as interpretability and modularity.

on (

questions.baseline_answer:
The step-by-step explanation of how a masked-language-model (MLM) pre-training objective can improve downstream open-domain question answering (Open-QA) in REALM is as follows:

1. **Latent Variable Modeling with Retrieval**  
   REALM models the task as a two-step generative process: first retrieve potentially useful documents from a large corpus, then predict the output (masked token or answer) conditioned on both the input and the retrieved documents. The retrieved documents are treated as latent variables in the model. The overall likelihood of generating the missing token (in pre-training) or the answer (in Open-QA) marginalizes over possible retrieved documents, integrating retrieval and prediction steps [5].

2. **Masked Language Model Objective with Retrieval Conditioning**  
   During pre-training, a sentence from the corpus is masked (input x) and the model is trained to predict the masked tokens (output y). Instead of predicting purely from the masked input, REALM conditions its prediction on documents retrieved from the corpus that are relevant to the masked input. This allows the language model to leverage external knowledge stored in the corpus rather than relying solely on its internal parameters [3][5].

3. **Learning Retrieval with a Performance-Based Signal**  
   The retriever is trained end-to-end with the language model, using the improvement in predicting masked tokens as the learning signal. If retrieving certain documents reduces perplexity for predicting masked tokens, the retriever is rewarded for selecting those documents. This encourages the retriever to learn to select documents that are indeed helpful for filling in the missing words, thereby aligning the retrieval step with the language modeling objective [2].

4. **Salient Span Masking and Unsupervised Text Signal**  
   REALM uses a specialized masking scheme called salient span masking that masks meaningful spans related to factual information, unlike random token or span masking schemes used in BERT or SpanBERT. This produces a consistent and informative learning signal that benefits the joint training of retriever and language model, making retrieval more effective for knowledge-requiring predictions [1].

5. **Marginalizing Over Multiple Retrieved Documents**  
   REALM does not rely on a single retrieved document but marginalizes over the top retrieved documents. This reduces the chance of missing relevant information and improves prediction accuracy for masked tokens by conditioning predictions on a richer set of retrieved knowledge [1].

6. **Improved Knowledge Encoding and Retrieval Coordination**  
   By pre-training in this retrieve-then-predict manner, both the retriever and the language model encoder learn representations
questions.orchestrator_answer:
The masked-language-model pre-training objective in REALM improves downstream open-domain question answering (QA) by integrating retrieval and language modeling in a multi-step process [E1][E4]. Specifically, REALM augments the conventional masked-language-model pre-training by incorporating a learned textual knowledge retriever that explicitly retrieves relevant documents from a large corpus such as Wikipedia before predicting masked tokens [E2][E3][E4]. Unlike traditional language models that store knowledge implicitly within their parameters, REALM explicitly exposes and leverages world knowledge during pre-training by conditioning token prediction on these retrieved documents [E2][E3].

During training, REALM applies a salient span masking scheme, which masks meaningful spans of text rather than random tokens or spans; this scheme provides a consistent and strong learning signal vital for the latent variable learning approach in REALM [E1]. Before each token prediction, the retriever fetches documents relevant to the masked context, and the language model attends over these retrieved documents to improve masked token prediction, thereby effectively incorporating external knowledge [E1][E4]. This process is optimized end-to-end with backpropagation through the retrieval step, rewarding retrievals that improve language model perplexity while penalizing uninformative ones [E4].

An example illustrating this shows that REALM retrieves documents containing relevant facts, which enables the model to assign a much higher probability (e.g., 0.129) to the correct masked token compared to a baseline model without retrieval whose probability can be near zero (e.g., 1.1 × 10^-14) [E1]. This joint training approach improves both the retriever and the encoder components; training either alone without the other leads to reduced gains, demonstrating the synergy of retrieval-augmented masked language modeling [E1].

Together, this retrieval-augmented masked-language-model pre-training objective trains REALM to learn which external texts improve language modeling and downstream tasks, leading to substantial improvements (e.g., 4-16% absolute accuracy gains) on open-domain QA benchmarks after fine-tuning [E4].

An explicit retrieval system allows us to adapt to new

the frequent mention of her name in Wikipedia articles.)

The null document ∅ described in Section
3.4 provides a
